title: Witch Week - Chapter 1 (Fragment 1)
text:
1

1

T HE NOTE SAID : SOMEONE IN THIS CLASS IS A WITCH. It was written in capital letters in ordinary blue ballpoint, and it had appeared between two of the geography books Mr. Crossley was marking. Anyone could have written it. Mr. Crossley rubbed his ginger moustache unhappily. He looked out over the bowed heads of Class 6B and wondered what to do about it.

He decided not to take the note to the headmistress. It was possibly just a joke, and Miss Cadwallader had no sense of humor to speak of. The person to take it to was the deputy head, Mr. Wentworth. But the difficulty there was that Mr. Wentworth’s son was a member of 6B—the small boy near the back who looked younger than the rest was Brian Wentworth. No. Mr. Crossley decided to ask the writer of the note to own up. He would explain just what a serious accusation it was and leave the rest to the person’s conscience.

Mr. Crossley cleared his throat to speak. Some of 6B looked up hopefully but Mr. Crossley had changed his mind then. It was journal time, and journal time was only to be interrupted for a serious emergency. Larwood House was very strict about that rule. Larwood House was very strict about a lot of things, because it was a boarding school run by the government for witch-orphans and children with other problems. The journals were to help the children with their problems. They were supposed to be strictly private. Every day, for half an hour, every pupil had to confide his or her private thoughts to their journals, and nothing else was done until everyone had. Mr. Crossley admired the idea heartily.

But the real reason that Mr. Crossley changed his mind was the awful thought that the note might be true. Someone in 6B could easily be a witch. Only Miss Cadwallader knew who exactly in 6B was a witch-orphan, but Mr. Crossley suspected that a lot of them were. Other classes had given Mr. Crossley feelings of pride and pleasure in being a schoolmaster; 6B never did. Only two of them gave him any pride at all: Theresa Mullett and Simon Silverson. They were both model pupils. The rest of the girls tailed dismally off until you came to empty chatterers like Estelle Green, or that dumpy girl, Nan Pilgrim, who was definitely the odd one out. The boys were divided into groups. Some had the sense to follow Simon Silverson’s example, but quite as many clustered around that bad boy Dan Smith, and others again admired that tall Indian boy Nirupam Singh. Or they were loners like Brian Wentworth and that unpleasant boy Charles Morgan.

Here Mr. Crossley looked at Charles Morgan and Charles Morgan looked back, with one of the blank, nasty looks he was famous for. Charles wore glasses, which enlarged the nasty look and trained it on Mr. Crossley like a double laser beam. Mr. Crossley looked away hastily and went back to worrying about the note. Everyone in 6B gave up hoping for anything interesting to happen and went back to their journals.

28 October 1981, Theresa Mullett wrote in round, angelic writing. Mr. Crossley has found a note in our geography books. I thought it might be from Miss Hodge at first, because we all know Teddy is dying for love of her, but he looks so worried that I think it must be from some silly girl like Estelle Green. Nan Pilgrim couldn’t get over the vaulting horse again today. She jumped and stuck halfway. It made us all laugh.

Simon Silverson wrote: 28. 10. 81. I would like to know who put that note in the geography books. It fell out when I was collecting them and I put it back in. If it was found lying about we could all be blamed. This is strictly off the record of course.

vocabulary.definition: A charge or claim that someone has done something wrong or illegal.
vocabulary.example: He would explain just what a serious accusation it was and leave the rest to the person’s conscience.

vocabulary.definition: Deserving respect or approval.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley admired the idea heartily.

vocabulary.definition: Resembling or characteristic of an angel, especially in purity or goodness.
vocabulary.example: Theresa Mullett wrote in round, angelic writing.

vocabulary.definition: Causing difficulty; hard to do or deal with.
vocabulary.example: The note was an awkward issue for Mr. Crossley to handle.

vocabulary.definition: Held responsible for a fault or wrong.
vocabulary.example: If it was found lying about we could all be blamed.

vocabulary.definition: Showing no expression or understanding; empty of expression.
vocabulary.example: Charles Morgan looked back with one of the blank, nasty looks he was famous for.

vocabulary.definition: A school where students live during the term time.
vocabulary.example: Larwood House was very strict because it was a boarding school run by the government for witch-orphans.

vocabulary.definition: Fill a space in a disorderly way (not directly in text but as conceptual related word to 'clustered').
vocabulary.example: The boys were divided into groups; some clustered around Simon Silverson’s example.

vocabulary.definition: Work together on an activity or project.
vocabulary.example: In a school environment, pupils sometimes collaborate on assignments.

vocabulary.definition: Gathered together; brought items to one place.
vocabulary.example: I was collecting the geography books when the note fell out.

vocabulary.definition: To tell someone a secret or private matter while trusting them not to repeat it to others.
vocabulary.example: Every pupil had to confide his or her private thoughts to their journals.

vocabulary.definition: An inner feeling of right and wrong.
vocabulary.example: He would leave the rest to the person’s conscience.

vocabulary.definition: A person who acts as second in command to the head of a school or organisation.
vocabulary.example: The note was to be taken to the deputy head, Mr Wentworth.

vocabulary.definition: Gloomy or depressing; showing lack of skill.
vocabulary.example: The rest of the girls tailed dismally off.

vocabulary.definition: A serious, unexpected situation requiring immediate action.
vocabulary.example: Journal time was only to be interrupted for a serious emergency.

vocabulary.definition: People who talk a lot without substance or meaningful content.
vocabulary.example: Girls like Estelle Green were empty chatterers.

vocabulary.definition: The study of Earth's physical features and human societies.
vocabulary.example: The note was found between two geography books.

vocabulary.definition: The group of people with the authority to govern a country or state.
vocabulary.example: The school was run by the government.

vocabulary.definition: Done with excessive speed or urgency; hurried.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley looked away hastily and went back to worrying about the note.

vocabulary.definition: The quality of being amusing or comic.
vocabulary.example: Miss Cadwallader had no sense of humor to speak of.

vocabulary.definition: To frighten or overawe someone, especially to make them do what one wants.
vocabulary.example: Charles Morgan's nasty looks seemed to intimidate Mr. Crossley.

vocabulary.definition: A daily record of personal experiences, thoughts, or events.
vocabulary.example: It was journal time, when pupils wrote private thoughts.

vocabulary.definition: A student who behaves well and works hard, setting an example for others.
vocabulary.example: Theresa Mullett and Simon Silverson were model pupils.

vocabulary.definition: Hair grown on the upper lip.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley rubbed his ginger moustache unhappily.

vocabulary.definition: Demanding that rules concerning behavior are obeyed and observed.
vocabulary.example: Larwood House was very strict about certain rules.

vocabulary.definition: To have an idea or impression of the existence or presence of something without certain proof.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley suspected that many pupils were witch-orphans.

vocabulary.definition: A gymnastic apparatus used for jumping and vaulting.
vocabulary.example: Nan Pilgrim couldn’t get over the vaulting horse again today.

vocabulary.definition: A child who has lost parents due to witch-related circumstances, central to the story's setting.
vocabulary.example: Larwood House was run for witch-orphans and children with problems.

vocabulary.definition: To feel anxious or concerned about something.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley was worried about the note.
